response: Global food production must rise by 60% to feed an estimated 9.7 billion people by 2050, even as climate change cuts crop yields through droughts, floods and extreme temperatures. Agriculture already accounts for about 25% of global greenhouse-gas emissions, water scarcity threatens major farming areas (for example, the Ogallala Aquifer is being depleted faster than it can recharge), and soil degradation has lowered productivity on 33% of arable land. Compounding these pressures, roughly one-third of all food is lost or wasted between farm and fork. Proposed solutions range from precision agriculture, vertical farming, alternative proteins and drought-resistant crops to regenerative practices and policy reforms on subsidies, trade and carbon pricing—challenges that must be scaled affordably for the world’s poorest.
